Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in New Hampshire: $120,000)

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in New Hampshire: $1,200 - $2,500 for Chapter 7, $3,000 - $5,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

## File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in New Jersey
Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/new-jersey.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in New Jersey

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in New Jersey. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for NJ self-represented filers.

Home › States › New Jersey

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in New Jersey?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in New Jersey. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in New Jersey

Bankruptcy cases in New Jersey are filed in the following federal districts:

District of New Jersey of New Jersey

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In New Jersey, the means test uses a median income of approximately $59,928 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in New Jersey and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: New Jersey allows you to choose federal or state exemptions. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in New Jersey: $0 (no homestead exemption))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in New Jersey: $1,500 - $3,000 for Chapter 7, $3,500 - $6,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/new-mexico.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home

Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in New Mexico

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in New Mexico. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for NM self-represented filers.

Home › States › New Mexico

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in New Mexico?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in New Mexico. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in New Mexico

Bankruptcy cases in New Mexico are filed in the following federal districts:

District of New Mexico of New Mexico

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In New Mexico, the means test uses a median income of approximately $40,784 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in New Mexico and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: New Mexico allows you to choose federal or state exemptions. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in New Mexico: $60,000)

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in New Mexico: $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $4,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

## File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in New York
Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/new-york.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in New York

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in New York. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for NY self-represented filers.

Home › States › New York

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in New York?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in New York. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in New York

Bankruptcy cases in New York are filed in the following federal districts:

Southern District of New York

Eastern District of New York

Northern District of New York

Western District of New York

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In New York, the means test uses a median income of approximately $55,107 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in New York and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: New York requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in New York: $179,950 - $399,975 (varies by county))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in New York: $1,500 - $3,500 for Chapter 7, $3,500 - $6,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in North Carolina

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in North Carolina. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for NC self-represented filers.

Home › States › North Carolina

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in North Carolina?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in North Carolina. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in North Carolina

Bankruptcy cases in North Carolina are filed in the following federal districts:

Eastern District of North Carolina

Middle District of North Carolina

Western District of North Carolina

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In North Carolina, the means test uses a median income of approximately $45,694 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in North Carolina and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: North Carolina requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in North Carolina: $35,000 ($60,000 if age 65+ or disabled))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in North Carolina: $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $4,500 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in North Dakota. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for ND self-represented filers.

Home › States › North Dakota

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in North Dakota?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in North Dakota. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Bankruptcy cases in North Dakota are filed in the following federal districts:

District of North Dakota of North Dakota

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in North Dakota: $150,000)

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in North Dakota: $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $4,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Ohio

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Ohio. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for OH self-represented filers.

Home › States › Ohio

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Ohio?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Ohio. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Ohio

Bankruptcy cases in Ohio are filed in the following federal districts:

Northern District of Ohio

Southern District of Ohio

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Ohio, the means test uses a median income of approximately $46,081 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Ohio and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Ohio requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Ohio: $145,425)

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Ohio: $1,000 - $2,500 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $4,500 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

## File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Oklahoma
Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/oklahoma.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Oklahoma

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Oklahoma. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for OK self-represented filers.

Home › States › Oklahoma

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Oklahoma?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Oklahoma. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Oklahoma

Bankruptcy cases in Oklahoma are filed in the following federal districts:

Northern District of Oklahoma

Eastern District of Oklahoma

Western District of Oklahoma

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Oklahoma, the means test uses a median income of approximately $43,077 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Oklahoma and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Oklahoma requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Oklahoma: Unlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Oklahoma: $800 - $1,800 for Chapter 7, $2,000 - $4,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

## File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Oregon
Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/oregon.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Oregon

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Oregon. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for OR self-represented filers.

Home › States › Oregon

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Oregon?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Oregon. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Oregon

Bankruptcy cases in Oregon are filed in the following federal districts:

District of Oregon of Oregon

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Oregon, the means test uses a median income of approximately $50,990 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Oregon and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Oregon allows you to choose federal or state exemptions. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Oregon: $40,000 ($50,000 joint))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Oregon: $1,200 - $2,500 for Chapter 7, $3,000 - $5,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

## File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Pennsylvania
Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/pennsylvania.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Pennsylvania

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Pennsylvania. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for PA self-represented filers.

Home › States › Pennsylvania

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Pennsylvania?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Pennsylvania. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Pennsylvania

Bankruptcy cases in Pennsylvania are filed in the following federal districts:

Eastern District of Pennsylvania

Middle District of Pennsylvania

Western District of Pennsylvania

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Pennsylvania, the means test uses a median income of approximately $49,990 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Pennsylvania and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Pennsylvania allows you to choose federal or state exemptions. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Pennsylvania: $0 (no homestead exemption under state law))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Pennsylvania: $1,200 - $2,500 for Chapter 7, $3,000 - $5,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Rhode Island

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Rhode Island. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for RI self-represented filers.

Home › States › Rhode Island

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Rhode Island?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Rhode Island. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Rhode Island

Bankruptcy cases in Rhode Island are filed in the following federal districts:

District of Rhode Island of Rhode Island

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Rhode Island, the means test uses a median income of approximately $52,210 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Rhode Island and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Rhode Island allows you to choose federal or state exemptions. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Rhode Island: $500,000)

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Rhode Island: $1,200 - $2,500 for Chapter 7, $3,000 - $5,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

## File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in South Carolina
Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/south-carolina.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in South Carolina

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in South Carolina. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for SC self-represented filers.

Home › States › South Carolina

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in South Carolina?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in South Carolina. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in South Carolina

Bankruptcy cases in South Carolina are filed in the following federal districts:

District of South Carolina of South Carolina

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In South Carolina, the means test uses a median income of approximately $42,705 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in South Carolina and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: South Carolina requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in South Carolina: $67,100 (real property) or $5,600 (personal property))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in South Carolina: $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $4,500 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in South Dakota. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for SD self-represented filers.

Home › States › South Dakota

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in South Dakota. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

District of South Dakota of South Dakota

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

In South Dakota, the means test uses a median income of approximately $47,805 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in South Dakota and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: South Dakota requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in South Dakota: Unlimited (1 acre urban, 160 acres rural))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in South Dakota: $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $4,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

## File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Tennessee
Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/tennessee.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Tennessee

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Tennessee. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for TN self-represented filers.

Home › States › Tennessee

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Tennessee?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Tennessee. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Tennessee

Bankruptcy cases in Tennessee are filed in the following federal districts:

Eastern District of Tennessee

Middle District of Tennessee

Western District of Tennessee

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Tennessee, the means test uses a median income of approximately $44,027 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Tennessee and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Tennessee requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Tennessee: $5,000 ($7,500 joint; $25,000 if age 62+))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Tennessee: $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $4,500 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

## File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Texas
Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/texas.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Home
Chapter 7
Chapter 13
Forms Guide
Common Mistakes
FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Texas

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Texas. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for TX self-represented filers.

Home › States › Texas

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Texas?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Texas. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Texas

Bankruptcy cases in Texas are filed in the following federal districts:

Northern District of Texas

Eastern District of Texas

Southern District of Texas

Western District of Texas

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Texas, the means test uses a median income of approximately $49,398 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Texas and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Texas requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Texas: Unlimited (10 acres urban, 100 acres rural / 200 family))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Texas: $1,000 - $2,500 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $5,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

Chapter 13

FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Utah

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Utah. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for UT self-represented filers.

Home › States › Utah

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Utah?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Utah. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Utah

Bankruptcy cases in Utah are filed in the following federal districts:

District of Utah of Utah

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Utah, the means test uses a median income of approximately $51,316 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Utah and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Utah requires state exemptions only. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Utah: $44,100 ($22,050 per individual))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Utah: $1,000 - $2,000 for Chapter 7, $2,500 - $4,500 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

Source: https://filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com/states/vermont.html

filebankruptcywithoutlawyer.com
☰

Chapter 13

FAQ
By State

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Vermont

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Vermont. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for VT self-represented filers.

Home › States › Vermont

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Vermont?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Vermont. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Vermont

Bankruptcy cases in Vermont are filed in the following federal districts:

District of Vermont of Vermont

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Required Forms and Documents

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Vermont, the means test uses a median income of approximately $49,211 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Vermont and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Vermont allows you to choose federal or state exemptions. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Vermont: $125,000)

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Vermont: $1,200 - $2,500 for Chapter 7, $3,000 - $5,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Virginia

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Virginia. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for VA self-represented filers.

Home › States › Virginia

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Virginia?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Virginia. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy Courts in Virginia

Bankruptcy cases in Virginia are filed in the following federal districts:

Eastern District of Virginia

Western District of Virginia

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Every bankruptcy filing requires a set of Official Bankruptcy Forms available free at uscourts.gov. The core forms include:

Voluntary Petition (Form 101): Initiates the case. Includes personal information, chapter choice, and attorney certification (or pro se declaration).

Schedules A/B through J: Property, secured/unsecured debts, executory contracts, co-debtors, income, and expenses.

Statement of Financial Affairs (Form 107): Detailed financial history including lawsuits, transfers, payments to creditors, and tax returns.

Means Test (Form 122A-1/2 for Ch. 7, Form 122C-1/2 for Ch. 13): Determines eligibility (Ch. 7) or disposable income (Ch. 13).

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Virginia, the means test uses a median income of approximately $54,376 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

Common Pro Se Mistakes

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Virginia and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Virginia allows you to choose federal or state exemptions. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Virginia: $25,000 ($50,000 for 65+))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Virginia: $1,200 - $2,500 for Chapter 7, $3,000 - $5,000 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.

Step-by-step guide to filing bankruptcy pro se in Washington. Court locations, filing fees, required forms, and common mistakes for WA self-represented filers.

Home › States › Washington

Can You File Bankruptcy Without a Lawyer in Washington?

Yes. There is no legal requirement to hire an attorney to file bankruptcy in Washington. You have a constitutional right to represent yourself (pro se) in federal bankruptcy court. However, bankruptcy is complex, and judges, trustees, and court staff cannot give you legal advice.

Filing pro se works best for straightforward Chapter 7 cases with few assets, no real property, and no business debt. Chapter 13 is significantly more complex because you must propose a repayment plan that satisfies the Bankruptcy Code's requirements.

Important: Even if you file pro se, you are held to the same legal standards as someone with an attorney. Mistakes on your petition, schedules, or means test can result in dismissal, denial of discharge, or even allegations of fraud.

Bankruptcy cases in Washington are filed in the following federal districts:

Eastern District of Washington

Western District of Washington

Each district maintains its own clerk's office, local rules, and filing procedures. Before filing pro se, download the local rules from your district's website. Local rules govern things like paper size, font requirements, number of copies, and hearing procedures that are unique to each district.

Filing fees: $338 for Chapter 7, $313 for Chapter 13. If you cannot afford the filing fee, you can request to pay in installments (Form 103A) or, for Chapter 7 only, request a fee waiver (Form 103B) if your income is below 150% of the federal poverty line.

Credit counseling certificate: Required from an approved provider before filing.

In Washington, the means test uses a median income of approximately $57,896 for a single filer. If your income is below this, you presumptively pass the Chapter 7 means test.

The most frequent mistakes pro se filers make in Washington and nationwide include:

Missing the 341 meeting: Your case will be dismissed if you fail to attend the meeting of creditors. It is mandatory.

Incorrect exemptions: Washington allows you to choose federal or state exemptions. Claiming the wrong exemption system can cost you property.

Incomplete schedules: Failing to list all assets or debts is grounds for denial of discharge under 11 U.S.C. Section 727(a)(4).

Missing deadlines: The bankruptcy code has strict deadlines for filing documents, attending hearings, and completing the debtor education course.

Not understanding the automatic stay: The stay protects you, but violations must be enforced through motion practice.

Pro tip: Attend a few public 341 meetings before filing your own case. They are open to the public and will give you a sense of what the trustee asks and how the process works in your district.

When to Consider Hiring an Attorney

While pro se filing is your right, certain situations strongly favor hiring an attorney:

You own a home with significant equity (homestead exemption in Washington: $125,000 (varies by county, up to $125K))

You have a business or business debts

You are facing a foreclosure, repossession, or garnishment

Your income is near or above the means test median

You have been sued or have pending litigation

You previously filed bankruptcy (timing rules apply under 11 U.S.C. Section 109(g) and 1328(f))

Typical attorney fees in Washington: $1,500 - $3,000 for Chapter 7, $3,500 - $5,500 for Chapter 13. Many attorneys offer free consultations.
